Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine most of the time. I'd kind of drift through the week and then realize I had stuff due soon. Sometimes I'd put on lecture recordings while doing other things, like eating or just lying on my bed staring at the ceiling. The 24 hours I spent studying each week were scattered and unfocused—I just couldn't bring myself to sit down and properly organize anything.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd have these bursts of activity when deadlines were breathing down my neck, but otherwise I'd let days slip by without touching course materials. Some weeks I'd tell myself I'd study every evening, but then I'd just feel too drained or anxious to actually do it. The regularity I wanted never really happened.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification on my phone and think 'I should probably check that.' Other times I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up just clicking around without really engaging. It was rarely because I actually felt motivated to learn.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were maybe a few weeks where I'd check in more regularly, but it never stuck as a habit. The mindfulness stuff they had us do helped a bit with my anxiety, and during that time I logged in more consistently. But as soon as that wore off, I drifted back to my usual pattern of avoiding it until I absolutely couldn't anymore.
Q5: The lecture recordings were probably the most useful because I could listen to them while doing other stuff or lying down when I felt too anxious to sit at a desk. I'm an auditory learner, so hearing explanations worked better for me than reading dense texts. The discussion posts were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that clicked better than the official materials.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just sat there in my downloads folder untouched. I think I was too overwhelmed to deal with walls of text, and my anxiety made it hard to focus on reading for long periods. The optional practice quizzes I mostly ignored too because they felt like extra pressure I didn't need.
Q7: If something felt like it would directly help me pass an exam or complete an assignment, I'd engage with it. Anything that seemed like busywork or 'enrichment' just felt like another weight on my shoulders. I needed to see an immediate payoff, otherwise my brain would just shut down and tell me it wasn't worth the energy. With everything else going on in my head, I had to be selective.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps, honestly. Like, they're supposed to be helpful, but they just added to my guilt when I didn't do them. Required stuff I'd force myself through because I had no choice, but optional things would sit there making me feel bad about myself for not being a 'good student.' The pressure was different—one was external, the other was this internal shame spiral.
Q9: Yeah, a lot actually. I'd download the lecture audio and listen to it on my phone when I was out walking or trying to calm down. I couldn't always be at my laptop, especially when my anxiety was bad. Sometimes I'd just lie in bed with headphones and replay lectures, trying to absorb information without the stress of being online and seeing all my incomplete tasks.
Q10: There were nights when I'd spend hours going over my handwritten notes or replaying downloaded lectures on my phone away from my computer. The LMS wouldn't know I was studying because I wasn't logged in. I'd also talk through concepts out loud to myself, which sounds weird, but it helped me process things. None of that showed up anywhere.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a lecture once and then listen to it multiple times over the next few days without ever logging back in. It was easier that way—less overwhelming than seeing the whole course interface with all its reminders of what I hadn't done. The offline studying felt safer somehow, like I could learn without being judged by the system.
Q12: There were stretches where my anxiety got really bad and I could barely function. I'd look at the LMS and feel this wave of dread wash over me. During those times, everything felt impossible—even simple tasks seemed insurmountable. My sadness would get heavy, and I'd just withdraw from everything, including the course. It wasn't that I didn't care; I just couldn't cope.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed about money or other life stuff, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd log in, see all the things I needed to do, and feel completely paralyzed. The fatigue made it hard to concentrate, and competing responsibilities—like trying to figure out how to afford things—took priority over watching another lecture video.
Q14: So many times. I'd have the LMS open, maybe even have a video playing, but I'd be completely zoned out. My mind would be somewhere else, worrying about other things or just feeling empty. I'd realize twenty minutes had passed and I couldn't remember anything that was said. It looked like engagement, but it was just me going through the motions.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important, but I'd keep putting it off because the thought of starting it made my chest tight. It's like my brain associated coursework with stress, so it would find any excuse to avoid it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd feel even worse about having waited.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd cram everything into the last possible window, working in this panicked state that wasn't healthy but got things done. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined otherwise. It wasn't a good system, but it was the only way I could function with my motivation being so low.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it was a mixed bag. Good feedback would give me a brief lift, but it wouldn't last. Critical feedback would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. I think I was too fragile to handle criticism properly, so I'd sometimes not even look at my grades because I was scared of what I'd see.
Q18: The structure felt both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules was good because I knew what existed, but seeing everything laid out made me realize how much I wasn't doing. The weekly format created this pressure that I couldn't keep up with. I needed more flexibility, but I also needed more structure—I don't know, it's complicated.
Q19: The fact that lectures were recorded and downloadable made things easier for me since I could study in my own way. But the discussion requirements felt forced and added to my anxiety about performing for others. The sheer number of resources available was actually paralyzing—I didn't know where to start, so sometimes I just didn't start at all.
Q20: I think that was literally me sometimes. I'd be clicking around, opening things, maybe watching bits of videos, but never actually completing the work. It's like I was trying to engage but couldn't follow through. Maybe it's anxiety, maybe it's not understanding what's expected, maybe it's just being overwhelmed. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That's kind of my situation, I guess. My exam scores were good because I could cram and perform under pressure, and I'm decent at absorbing information through listening. But my day-to-day LMS activity was low because I did a lot of studying offline and avoided the platform when my anxiety was high. The numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you had them open, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything or were just staring blankly at the screen. They miss all the offline work, the mental struggles, the times you're learning in ways that don't involve a computer. They're like a shadow of engagement, not the real thing.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just completing tasks—it's wanting to understand, being curious, feeling like the material matters. I struggled with that this semester because my mental state made it hard to care about anything. I was going through the motions more than truly engaging.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I was struggling internally while still managing to get decent grades. It can't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures on my phone during walks to manage my anxiety, or the nights I couldn't sleep because I was worried about falling behind. It doesn't know about the sadness that made everything feel pointless, or how hard I had to fight just to submit anything at all.
